frameworks.name: event segmentation theory
frameworks.one_line: Salient events trigger boundary signals that update episodic memory.

frameworks.name: deviance detection / oddball
frameworks.one_line: Salience treated as deviant-stimulus detection rather than memory update.

frameworks.name: prefrontal-hippocampal memory network
frameworks.one_line: PFC-hippocampus circuit binds temporal and spatial associations of events.

core_question: Does increased neural activity to salient events reflect deviance detection or a memory-update signal, and do distinct regions track distinct sub-types of saliency?
key_assumptions: Saliency can be decomposed into value, task-relevance, and event-boundary components.
PFC-hippocampus network jointly handles event associations and deviant detection.
Oddball-in-stream paradigm dissociates these saliency sub-types.
manipulations: Event-value saliency (reward/value of items in the stream).
Task-relevance saliency (whether deviants matter for the task).
Surprise-driven event-boundary saliency (unexpected transitions in temporal context).
key_findings: Medial OFC activity tracked event-value saliency.
Dorsolateral PFC activity tracked task-relevance saliency.
Hippocampus activity tracked surprise-driven event-boundary saliency.
interpretations: The PFC-hippocampus network factorizes saliency into value, task-relevance, and event-boundary components.
Distinct regions implement dissociable sub-types of saliency rather than a unitary deviance signal.
